name: Argentina
summary: Argentina is known for high-altitude viticulture producing ripe yet balanced wines, especially Malbec.
examTips: Altitude is one of the most important themes in Argentina.
Malbec is the flagship grape to remember.
Think ripe fruit balanced by freshness from elevation and diurnal range.

mendoza.name: Mendoza (General)
mendoza.summary: Argentina’s most important wine region, known for ripe wines balanced by altitude and diurnal range.
mendoza.styleSummary: Ripe fruit wines with freshness from altitude and diurnal range.
examTips: Mendoza is the core region for understanding Argentine wine.
Altitude and Andes meltwater irrigation are key themes.

Malbec.summary: Argentina’s flagship red grape, producing ripe black-fruited wines with floral notes and soft to medium tannin.
Malbec.style: Black fruit, plum, violet, soft tannin with full body.
examTips: Black fruit plus violet is a strong clue.
Argentine Malbec is usually riper and fuller than Cahors Malbec.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Structured Bordeaux variety producing blackcurrant fruit, firm tannin and full body in warm continental sites.
Cabernet Sauvignon.style: Blackcurrant, spice, structured with firm tannin.
examTips: Usually more structured and firmer than Malbec.

maipu.name: Maipu
maipu.summary: Historic warmer Mendoza sub-region known for ripe generous red wines.
maipu.styleSummary: Ripe fruit-driven Malbec and Cabernet styles with fuller body.
examTips: Warmth gives a riper profile than the highest coolest Mendoza sites.
Altitude still helps preserve acidity.

Malbec.summary: Argentina’s flagship red grape, producing ripe black-fruited wines with floral notes and soft to medium tannin.
Malbec.style: Black fruit, plum, violet, soft tannin with full body.
examTips: Black fruit plus violet is a strong clue.
Argentine Malbec is usually riper and fuller than Cahors Malbec.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Structured Bordeaux variety producing blackcurrant fruit, firm tannin and full body in warm continental sites.
Cabernet Sauvignon.style: Blackcurrant, spice, structured with firm tannin.
examTips: Usually more structured and firmer than Malbec.

maipu.classification: Warmer historic sub-region producing riper fuller styles.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude lowers temperature and can extend the growing season, helping preserve acidity and aroma.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Growers often use elevation and harvest timing strategically to balance ripeness and freshness.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.

east-mendoza.name: East Mendoza
east-mendoza.summary: Lower flatter Mendoza area associated more with larger-volume production and warmer conditions.
east-mendoza.styleSummary: Riper softer styles with less concentration than the best high-altitude zones.
examTips: Useful as a contrast to the best premium upland Mendoza areas.
Warm and productive are key ideas.

Malbec.summary: Argentina’s flagship red grape, producing ripe black-fruited wines with floral notes and soft to medium tannin.
Malbec.style: Black fruit, plum, violet, soft tannin with full body.
examTips: Black fruit plus violet is a strong clue.
Argentine Malbec is usually riper and fuller than Cahors Malbec.

east-mendoza.classification: Lower warmer more productive section with less premium prestige than the top upland zones.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude lowers temperature and can extend the growing season, helping preserve acidity and aroma.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Growers often use elevation and harvest timing strategically to balance ripeness and freshness.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.

mendoza.classification: Mendoza is Argentina’s flagship region, with altitude and irrigation as the essential study concepts.
lawNotes: Altitude strongly affects freshness and concentration.
Irrigation from Andean meltwater is essential in this desert climate.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Mountain influence or rain-shadow effects can reduce rainfall and create cooler nights or sheltered mesoclimates.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Growers rely on site selection and exposure to exploit shelter and reduce climatic risk.

lujan-de-cuyo.name: Luján de Cuyo
lujan-de-cuyo.summary: Classic Mendoza subregion known for rich, ripe and structured Malbec.
lujan-de-cuyo.styleSummary: Classic Malbec region with rich, ripe and structured wines.
examTips: Think classic richer Mendoza Malbec.
Usually broader and softer than cooler higher-altitude examples.

Malbec.summary: Richer-style Argentine Malbec with ripe black fruit, fuller body and softer acidity.
Malbec.style: Ripe black fruit, chocolate, fuller body, softer acidity.
examTips: Richer and broader than higher-altitude Uco Valley examples.

vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Subregional site differences are important to how grapes ripen and to the final balance of the wines.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

uco-valley.name: Uco Valley
uco-valley.summary: High-altitude Mendoza subregion producing fresher, more elegant and structured wines.
uco-valley.styleSummary: More elegant, fresher wines with higher acidity and structure.
examTips: Altitude is the key clue here.
Expect more freshness and structure than lower Mendoza zones.

Malbec.summary: Higher-altitude Malbec with more freshness, floral lift and structure than warmer lowland styles.
Malbec.style: Black fruit with floral notes, higher acidity, more structure.
viticulture: High UV leads to thick skins
examTips: Higher acidity and structure are key differences versus Luján de Cuyo.

Chardonnay.summary: Fresh high-altitude Chardonnay with citrus, mineral notes and possible subtle oak.
Chardonnay.style: Fresh citrus, mineral, sometimes with subtle oak.
examTips: Altitude helps preserve acidity despite strong sunlight.

vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude lowers temperature and can extend the growing season, helping preserve acidity and aroma.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Growers often use elevation and harvest timing strategically to balance ripeness and freshness.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.

salta.name: Salta
salta.summary: Extreme high-altitude region known for highly aromatic whites and concentrated reds.
salta.styleSummary: Very aromatic whites and concentrated reds due to extreme altitude.
examTips: Salta is strongly associated with extreme altitude.
Torrontes is the key aromatic white grape here.

Torrontes.summary: Argentina’s signature aromatic white grape, producing floral, grapey and perfumed wines.
Torrontes.style: Highly aromatic, floral, lychee, grapey notes.
examTips: Highly aromatic profile is the key clue.
Usually made in fresh styles rather than oak-driven styles.

Malbec.summary: Extreme-altitude Malbec giving concentrated fruit and stronger structure.
Malbec.style: Very concentrated black fruit with strong structure.
examTips: More intense and concentrated than many Mendoza examples.

cafayate.name: Cafayate
cafayate.summary: High-altitude Salta sub-region famous for aromatic Torrontes and also Malbec.
cafayate.styleSummary: Very aromatic whites and deeply colored fresh reds thanks to altitude.
examTips: Altitude is the essential clue here.
Cafayate is the key named Salta place for Level 3.

Torrontes.summary: Argentina’s signature aromatic white grape, producing floral, grapey and perfumed wines.
Torrontes.style: Highly aromatic, floral, lychee, grapey notes.
examTips: Highly aromatic profile is the key clue.
Usually made in fresh styles rather than oak-driven styles.

Malbec.summary: Extreme-altitude Malbec giving concentrated fruit and stronger structure.
Malbec.style: Very concentrated black fruit with strong structure.
examTips: More intense and concentrated than many Mendoza examples.

cafayate.classification: Benchmark very high-altitude Salta sub-region for Torrontes and Malbec.

Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.

Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.

salta.classification: Salta is especially important for very high-altitude viticulture and Torrontes.
lawNotes: Cafayate is the key named place to remember.
Altitude gives aromatic intensity and freshness despite strong sunshine.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.

patagonia.name: Patagonia
patagonia.summary: Cooler southern region producing elegant, fresh wines with good acidity.
patagonia.styleSummary: Cooler region producing more elegant and fresh wines.
examTips: Think cooler climate and more elegance.
Patagonia is useful for comparing Argentina’s warmer and cooler styles.

Pinot Noir.summary: Cooler-climate red grape producing elegant red-fruited wines with fresher acidity.
Pinot Noir.style: Red cherry, raspberry, light body, fresh acidity.
examTips: Think lighter body and fresher profile than Malbec.

Chardonnay.summary: Cooler-climate Chardonnay producing fresh citrus and green apple styles.
Chardonnay.style: Citrus, green apple, fresh and balanced.
examTips: Cooler Argentine Chardonnay often shows fresher citrus character.

rio-negro.name: Rio Negro
rio-negro.summary: Cool southern Argentine area known for fresh Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.
rio-negro.styleSummary: Fresh lighter-bodied wines with bright acidity and restrained fruit.
examTips: Patagonia is cooler than the classic northern Argentine regions.
Pinot Noir is a key clue.

Pinot Noir.summary: Cooler-climate red grape producing elegant red-fruited wines with fresher acidity.
Pinot Noir.style: Red cherry, raspberry, light body, fresh acidity.
examTips: Think lighter body and fresher profile than Malbec.

Chardonnay.summary: Cooler-climate Chardonnay producing fresh citrus and green apple styles.
Chardonnay.style: Citrus, green apple, fresh and balanced.
examTips: Cooler Argentine Chardonnay often shows fresher citrus character.

rio-negro.classification: Classic cool Patagonian valley for fresh Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.
vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Wind can cool vineyards and reduce fungal disease pressure, but can also affect vigor and fruit set.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Training systems, row orientation, and site protection matter where wind is a major factor.

neuquen.name: Neuquen
neuquen.summary: Patagonian area with cool dry conditions supporting fresh modern styles.
neuquen.styleSummary: Bright fresh wines with moderate body and clean fruit.
examTips: Cool dry southern climate is the key context.
A useful Patagonia name to recognize on the map.

Pinot Noir.summary: Cooler-climate red grape producing elegant red-fruited wines with fresher acidity.
Pinot Noir.style: Red cherry, raspberry, light body, fresh acidity.
examTips: Think lighter body and fresher profile than Malbec.

Chardonnay.style: Citrus, green apple, fresh and balanced.

Rio Negro is the key named valley to recognize.
